title: U.S. Federal Budget
desc: How the government spent your money in 2025
details: This is the full picture of how the federal government spent money in 2025. It brings the biggest parts of the budget together in one place so you can see where the money went. If you enter your income, the app also gives you a rough estimate of how much of that spending your taxes supported.

categories.title: Health
categories.desc: Federal health spending, led by Medicare, Medicaid, marketplace aid, and public health agencies
categories.details: This is the big bucket for federal health spending. It includes programs like Medicare, Medicaid, marketplace subsidies, and public health agencies. Most of the money here goes toward health coverage and medical care, while a smaller share supports research, oversight, and public health work.

categories.title: Medicare
categories.desc: Insurance benefits and administration for seniors and people with qualifying disabilities
categories.details: This is the federal health insurance program for older adults and some people with disabilities. It helps pay for hospital stays, doctor visits, prescription drugs, and other care through different parts of the program. The total cost is offset by about -$200 billion in premiums, state payments, drug manufacturer rebates, and other offsets that reduce the net cost to taxpayers.

categories.title: Hospital Insurance (Part A)
categories.desc: Hospital insurance and inpatient care
categories.details: Pays for inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care (limited time), hospice, and some home health. If a patient breaks a hip and needs surgery plus 2 weeks of rehab at a nursing facility, Part A covers that.

categories.title: Medical Insurance (Part B)
categories.desc: Physician, outpatient, and related services
categories.details: Pays for doctor visits, outpatient hospital care, lab tests, preventive screenings (mammograms, colonoscopies), durable medical equipment (wheelchairs, oxygen), and physician-administered drugs (like chemotherapy infusions).

categories.title: Prescription Drug Benefit (Part D)
categories.desc: Prescription drug benefit
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on prescription drug benefit. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people are covered, what care they use, and how much that care costs.

categories.title: Administration & Other
categories.desc: Program administration and other Medicare spending
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Medicare behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, Medicare Integrity Program, and other behind-the-scenes work.

categories.title: Medicaid
categories.desc: Joint federal-state health coverage for low-income families, seniors, and people with disabilities
categories.details: Federal matching funds sent to states to reimburse them for covered medical services and administration. This pays for health coverage for ~70 million enrollees. The numbers in this section are estimates of the federal share of Medicaid spending, which is about 60% of the total cost.

categories.title: Managed Care
categories.desc: Medicaid managed care payments to health plans
categories.details: Instead of paying doctors and hospitals directly for every single service, the federal government pays a private health insurance company a fixed monthly amount for each Medicaid enrollee. The insurance company then handles all the care for that person.  The theory is that a health plan has an incentive to keep people healthy (preventive care, managing chronic conditions), therefore reducing costs and improving care because they pocket the savings if enrollees stay out of the hospital.

categories.title: Acute Care
categories.desc: Fee-for-Service (FFS) Acute Care
categories.details: The government pays a provider directly for each specific service delivered. One doctor visit = one payment. One surgery = one payment. No middleman health plan involved.

categories.title: Long Term Care
categories.desc: Long-term services and supports (LTSS)
categories.details: Care for people who can't fully care for themselves due to age, disability, or chronic illness. This includes nursing facility and home- and community-based services. Medicare does not cover long-term nursing home stays beyond 100 days, so Medicaid is the primary payer of long-term care in the US.

categories.title: Dual Eligibles
categories.desc: Medicare premiums and other support for dual-eligible enrollees
categories.details: About 12 million Americans are enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid simultaneously. Medicare is their primary insurance (it pays first), but it still has significant out-of-pocket costs — premiums, deductibles, and copays. Medicaid covers those gaps. Mostly elderly people who outlived their savings, and non-elderly people with significant disabilities (like those on SSI). They represent about 15% of Medicaid enrollment but a disproportionately large share of spending because they tend to be the sickest and most expensive enrollees.

categories.title: Administration & Other
categories.desc: State administration, program integrity, and other Medicaid costs
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Medicaid behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. Typically matched by the states at 50%.

categories.title: Marketplace Subsidies
categories.desc: ACA exchange subsidies that lower premiums and out-of-pocket costs
categories.details: This part of the budget covers ACA exchange subsidies that lower premiums and out-of-pocket costs. That includes things like Premium Tax Credits, Cost-Sharing Reductions, and Basic Health Program & Other.

categories.title: Premium Tax Credits
categories.desc: Advance premium tax credits for exchange plans
categories.details: The core subsidy. Paid directly to insurers each month on behalf of enrollees. Covers the gap between what a plan costs and what a household can afford to pay (capped as a % of income). In 2025, 22.4 million enrollees (92% of all marketplace participants) received advance premium tax credits.

categories.title: Cost-Sharing Reductions
categories.desc: Cost-sharing assistance for eligible enrollees
categories.details: Reduces what low-income silver-plan enrollees pay out-of-pocket (deductibles, copays, coinsurance). Available to households earning 100-250% of the federal poverty level (FPL) who enroll in silver plans. CSRs help defray cost-sharing costs (deductibles, copays, and coinsurance) for enrollees in silver plans — separate from PTCs, which subsidize premiums.

categories.title: Basic Health Program & Other
categories.desc: Basic Health Program payments and related marketplace support
categories.details: Lets states create a coverage option for people earning 138–200% FPL who would otherwise be eligible for marketplace plans. Federal funding to each state equals 95% of the amount in subsidies for which enrollees would otherwise have been eligible through a marketplace.

categories.title: Other Health Agencies
categories.desc: Public health agencies, research, mental health programs, and Tribal health services

categories.title: NIH
categories.desc: National Institutes of Health biomedical research
categories.details: This pays for research grants, labs, and clinical trials. It supports studies that led to new treatments for cancer, heart disease, and diabetes so patients nationwide can access better medicines.

categories.title: CDC
categories.desc: Center for Disease Control surveillance, preparedness, and prevention
categories.details: The CDC does disease tracking, vaccine programs, and outbreak response teams. It helps local health departments stop flu, COVID, and other illnesses before they spread to families.

categories.title: Indian Health Service
categories.desc: Health services for Tribal communities
categories.details: This pays for hospitals, clinics, and health services for American Indian and Alaska Native communities. It covers check-ups, emergency care, and diabetes treatment so patients can get care closer to home.

categories.title: SAMHSA
categories.desc: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration
categories.details: This pays for mental health and substance use treatment programs. It supports counseling and recovery services so people with addiction or depression can get help in their communities.

categories.title: FDA
categories.desc: Food and Drug Administration
categories.details: This pays for food and drug safety inspections and reviews. It ensures medicines and groceries on store shelves meet safety standards and protect families from unsafe products. An additional $3.5 billion in user fees comes from the industries the FDA regulates, which helps fund their work without relying solely on taxpayer dollars.

categories.title: HRSA
categories.desc: Health Resources and Services Administration
categories.details: This pays for community health centers and workforce training. It helps underserved areas have doctors and nurses for prenatal care and chronic disease management.

categories.title: AHRQ
categories.desc: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
categories.details: The federal agency charged with supporting research designed to improve the quality of health care, increase the efficiency of its delivery, and broaden access to health services. In addition, AHRQ is required to disseminate its research findings to health care providers, payers, and consumers, among others

This part of the budget covers public health agencies, research, mental health programs, and tribal health services. That includes things like NIH, CDC, and Indian Health Service, and other related programs. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people are covered, what care they use, and how much that care costs.

categories.title: CHIP
categories.desc: Children's Health Insurance Program
categories.details: Federal support that helps states insure children above Medicaid eligibility. States contribute an additional $6 billion. This covers around 9.5 million children.

categories.title: State CHIP Grants
categories.desc: Federal matching grants for state CHIP coverage
categories.details: The core of CHIP: federal matching dollars states use to pay doctors, hospitals, and health plans for children's care. Covers checkups, vaccines, dental, vision, emergency care, and prescriptions. It covers around 9.5 million children in families that earn too much for Medicaid but still need help affording coverage.

categories.title: Quality & Outreach
categories.desc: Quality, outreach, and enrollment support
categories.details: Discretionary grants to states, nonprofits, and community organizations to find and enroll eligible children who aren't yet covered. The Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 provided $48M for FY2024–2027 (~$12M/year).

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Program administration and oversight
categories.details: This is the money needed to run CHIP behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. An additional $350 million came from the states.

categories.title: Social Security
categories.desc: Cash benefits for retirees, survivors, and disabled workers through the Social Security system

categories.title: Old-Age & Survivors Insurance
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for retirees plus survivor support for spouses, children, and other family members

categories.title: Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly checks for retired workers
categories.details: This is the part of Social Security most people think of as retirement benefits. About 50 million retired workers get a check every month. The amount someone receives depends mostly on how much they earned over their working years and the age when they start claiming benefits.

categories.title: Spouses of Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for spouses of retired workers
categories.details: Monthly checks to about 2.1 million spouses (mostly wives who earned less or didn't work outside the home). This helps couples where one partner had little or no earnings history still have household income in retirement.

categories.title: Children of Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for children of retired workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to minor or disabled adult children of retirees, including cases like grandparents raising grandchildren or adult children disabled since childhood. Approximately 735,000 recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Aged Widow(er)s and Parents
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for aged widow(er)s and parents
categories.details: Monthly checks go to surviving spouses, mostly widows age 60+, helping prevent a total loss of household income after a worker dies. Approximately 3.5 million recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Children of Deceased Workers
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for children of deceased workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to approximately 2.0 million recipients children whose parent died, supporting housing, food, and school supplies.

categories.title: Survivors - Widowed Mothers/Fathers
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for widowed mothers and fathers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to surviving parents caring for a deceased worker's child under 16, allowing reduced work hours or caregiving at home. Approximately 99,000 recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Disabled Widow(er)s
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for disabled widow(er)s ages 50-59
categories.details: Monthly checks go to disabled surviving spouses ages 50-59, supporting people facing both bereavement and disability. Approximately 191,000 recipients.

categories.title: OASI Administrative Costs
categories.desc: SSA operations and claims processing for OASI
categories.details: This covers SSA staff, field offices, call center operations, IT systems, and claims processing. SSA operating expenses across programs are about 0.8% of benefits paid, one of the lowest admin-cost ratios of any large benefit program.

This part of the budget covers monthly benefits for retirees plus survivor support for spouses, children, and other family members. That includes things like Retired Workers, Spouses & Children, and Survivor Benefits, and other related programs. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people qualify and the size of the monthly benefits they receive.

categories.title: Disability Insurance
categories.desc: Income support for workers and dependents when a qualifying disability prevents work

categories.title: Disabled Workers
categories.desc: Benefits for disabled insured workers
categories.details: Monthly cash payments to ~7.2 million disabled workers. This is the primary income source for people unable to work due to a serious medical condition — paying rent, utilities, food, and medication.

categories.title: Dependents
categories.desc: Benefits for spouses and children of disabled workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to spouses and children of disabled workers, providing financial support when a worker is unable to work due to a disability.

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Administrative costs of the disability insurance program
categories.details: Covers ~58,000 SSA employees, 1,500+ field offices, disability determination services in all 50 states, and continuing disability reviews.
This part of the budget covers income support for workers and dependents when a qualifying disability prevents work. That includes things like Disabled Workers, Dependents, and Administration.

categories.title: Supplemental Security Income
categories.desc: Cash assistance for low-income seniors and people with disabilities

categories.title: Disabled Adults
categories.desc: Benefits for disabled adult recipients
categories.details: Monthly cash for working-age adults who can't hold a job due to severe physical or mental disabilities — covers rent, food, bus fare. About 60% have a mental health diagnosis.

categories.title: Disabled Children
categories.desc: Benefits for eligible disabled children
categories.details: Cash for families raising children with severe disabilities (autism, intellectual disabilities, other serious conditions). Helps cover costs of care, therapy co-pays, specialized food/clothing. Kids get higher average payments because their families typically have little other income.

categories.title: Aged
categories.desc: Benefits for aged recipients
categories.details: These are people who originally qualified as disabled before turning 65. SSI still classifies them as "disabled" even after 65.

categories.title: Blind
categories.desc: Benefits for blind recipients
categories.details: Monthly cash for people with statutory blindness (20/200 vision or worse). A very small share of the program. Blind recipients have slightly higher work-incentive thresholds.

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Administrative costs and related program support
categories.details: Pays SSA field office staff who process applications, state Disability Determination Services (DDS) workers who review medical evidence, rent on ~1,200 field offices, the national 800-number, and IT systems. This is how applications get decided.

categories.title: Program Integrity
categories.desc: Efforts to prevent and recover improper payments
categories.details: Funds continuing disability reviews (CDRs) and redeterminations to verify recipients still qualify. Catches overpayments and prevents fraud. SSA estimates these reviews save roughly $9 for every $1 spent.

This part of the budget covers cash assistance for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. That includes things like Disabled Adults, Disabled Children, and Aged & Blind, and other related programs. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

This is the part of the budget that sends monthly Social Security benefits to retirees, survivors, and disabled workers. It also covers the smaller amount needed to run those programs. Most of the spending here is driven by how many people qualify and the size of the benefits they receive.

categories.title: Net Interest
categories.desc: What the government pays to service federal debt after trust-fund offsets

categories.title: Interest on Public Debt
categories.desc: Interest owed to investors and institutions that hold Treasury securities

categories.title: Marketable Treasuries
categories.desc: Interest on notes, bonds, and bills held by the public
categories.details: This is the interest the government pays on notes, bonds, and bills held by the public. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The cost here mainly moves with the size of the national debt, interest rates, and inflation.

categories.title: Savings Bonds & Other
categories.desc: Interest on savings securities and other debt instruments
categories.details: This is the interest the government pays on savings securities and other debt instruments. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The cost here mainly moves with the size of the national debt, interest rates, and inflation.

categories.title: Inflation Adjustments
categories.desc: Inflation compensation and related debt adjustments
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on inflation compensation and related debt adjustments. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The cost here mainly moves with the size of the national debt, interest rates, and inflation.

This part of the budget covers interest owed to investors and institutions that hold treasury securities. That includes things like Marketable Treasuries, Savings Bonds & Other, and Inflation Adjustments. The cost here mainly moves with the size of the national debt, interest rates, and inflation.

categories.title: Interest Received by Trust Funds
categories.desc: Interest credited to federal trust funds that partially offsets gross debt costs

categories.title: Social Security Trust Funds
categories.desc: Interest received by the OASDI trust funds
categories.details: This is the interest the government credits to the OASDI trust funds. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The amount here depends on how much these trust funds hold in Treasury securities and the interest credited to them.

categories.title: Medicare Trust Funds
categories.desc: Interest received by Medicare trust funds
categories.details: This is the interest the government credits to medicare trust funds. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The amount here depends on how much these trust funds hold in Treasury securities and the interest credited to them.

categories.title: Civil Service & Other Trust Funds
categories.desc: Interest received by other federal trust funds
categories.details: This is the interest the government credits to other federal trust funds. This is not a program people sign up for. It is a financing cost the government owes because it has borrowed money. The amount here depends on how much these trust funds hold in Treasury securities and the interest credited to them.

This part of the budget covers interest credited to federal trust funds that partially offsets gross debt costs. That includes things like Social Security Trust Funds, Medicare Trust Funds, and Civil Service & Other Trust Funds. The amount here depends on how much these trust funds hold in Treasury securities and the interest credited to them.

This is the cost of carrying the federal debt. It is money the government owes to bondholders and other holders of Treasury securities, after backing out interest credited to certain trust funds. The size of this cost depends mostly on how much the government has borrowed, interest rates, and inflation.

categories.title: National Defense
categories.desc: Military operations, nuclear forces, and other defense-wide national security spending

categories.title: DoD Military Programs
categories.desc: Core Pentagon spending for readiness, troops, weapons, military construction, and research
categories.details: This is the main pot of Pentagon spending. It covers troop pay, training, weapons, military construction, and the everyday cost of running the armed forces. The total here depends mostly on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Operations & Maintenance
categories.desc: Training, readiness, sustainment, and day-to-day operations
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on training, readiness, sustainment, and day-to-day operations. It pays for the day-to-day work needed to keep this part of government or the program running. The amount here mainly depends on troop levels, pay tables, and the cost of benefits for service members and their families.

categories.title: Military Personnel
categories.desc: Pay, allowances, and benefits for service members
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on pay, allowances, and benefits for service members. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Procurement
categories.desc: Weapons systems, equipment, and related acquisitions
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on weapons systems, equipment, and related acquisitions. In practice, it pays for buildings, equipment, long-term projects, or physical infrastructure. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: RDT&E
categories.desc: Research, development, test, and evaluation
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on research, development, test, and evaluation. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

categories.title: Military Construction & Housing
categories.desc: Military construction, family housing, and other support accounts
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on military construction, family housing, and other support accounts. In practice, it pays for buildings, equipment, long-term projects, or physical infrastructure. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Atomic Energy Defense
categories.desc: Nuclear weapons stewardship, naval reactors, and cleanup of defense nuclear sites

categories.title: Weapons Activities
categories.desc: NNSA nuclear weapons stockpile and modernization work
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on NNSA nuclear weapons stockpile and modernization work. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Naval Reactors
categories.desc: Reactors and propulsion for the Navy nuclear fleet
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on reactors and propulsion for the navy nuclear fleet. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Defense Environmental Cleanup
categories.desc: Cleanup of legacy nuclear weapons sites
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on cleanup of legacy nuclear weapons sites. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Other Nuclear Security
categories.desc: Safeguards, security, and other defense nuclear programs
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on safeguards, security, and other defense nuclear programs. It pays for the day-to-day work needed to keep this part of government or the program running. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

This part of the budget covers nuclear weapons stewardship, naval reactors, and cleanup of defense nuclear sites. That includes things like Weapons Activities, Naval Reactors, and Defense Environmental Cleanup, and other related programs. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Other Defense Activities
categories.desc: Defense-wide agencies, intelligence support, and smaller national security accounts

categories.title: Defense-Wide Support
categories.desc: Defense-wide support agencies and command support
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on defense-wide support agencies and command support. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Intelligence & Security
categories.desc: Defense intelligence and related security activities
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on defense intelligence and related security activities. It pays for the day-to-day work needed to keep this part of government or the program running. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Other Defense Activities
categories.desc: Selective service, support funds, and other defense-related programs
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on selective service, support funds, and other defense-related programs. It pays for the day-to-day work needed to keep this part of government or the program running. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

This part of the budget covers defense-wide agencies, intelligence support, and smaller national security accounts. That includes things like Defense-Wide Support, Intelligence & Security, and Other Defense Activities. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

This is the big bucket for military and national security spending. It includes the armed forces, nuclear weapons work, and other defense-wide activities that support national security. The total here depends mostly on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the broader defense budget set by Congress.

categories.title: Income Security
categories.desc: Cash aid, tax credits, food aid, housing help, and unemployment support for households

categories.title: Child Tax Credit
categories.desc: Tax relief for families with children, much of it delivered through refunds

categories.title: Refundable CTC
categories.desc: Refundable child tax credit payments
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on refundable child tax credit payments. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Nonrefundable CTC
categories.desc: Offsetting tax liability through the nonrefundable portion
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on offsetting tax liability through the nonrefundable portion. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: IRS administration and compliance associated with the credit
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Child Tax Credit behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. The amount here depends more on workload, staffing, technology, and oversight needs than on direct payments to the public.

This is the tax credit that helps families with children keep more of their income. It usually shows up by reducing a tax bill, increasing a refund, or both. The total here depends on how many families qualify and how large their credits are under the current rules.

categories.title: SNAP (Food Stamps)
categories.desc: Nutrition assistance that helps low-income households buy groceries

categories.title: Food Benefits
categories.desc: Monthly nutrition benefits delivered to households
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on monthly nutrition benefits delivered to households. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

categories.title: State Administration
categories.desc: State administrative and systems costs
categories.details: This is the money needed to run SNAP (Food Stamps) behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. The amount here depends more on workload, staffing, technology, and oversight needs than on direct payments to the public.

categories.title: Employment & Training
categories.desc: SNAP employment and training support
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on SNAP employment and training support. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

This is the program many people still call food stamps. It helps lower-income households buy groceries each month through an electronic benefit card. The total here depends mostly on how many households qualify and how large their monthly food benefits are.

categories.title: Other Income Security
categories.desc: Family assistance, foster care, utility help, and other anti-poverty support

categories.title: TANF
categories.desc: Temporary Assistance for Needy Families block grants
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on temporary assistance for needy families block grants. A lot of this money flows out as grants or subsidies to states, local governments, schools, agencies, or other organizations. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Foster Care & Adoption
categories.desc: Foster care, adoption assistance, and guardianship support
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on foster care, adoption assistance, and guardianship support. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Energy & Utility Aid
categories.desc: Low Income Home Energy Assistance and similar support
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on low income home energy assistance and similar support. A lot of this money flows out as grants or subsidies to states, local governments, schools, agencies, or other organizations. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Child Support Enforcement
categories.desc: Federal child support and family support enforcement
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on federal child support and family support enforcement. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Other Assistance
categories.desc: Other family support and safety-net programs
categories.details: This bucket groups smaller assistance programs that still add up to real spending. It usually pulls together smaller costs in this area that are real parts of the budget even if they are not well known on their own. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

This part of the budget covers family assistance, foster care, utility help, and other anti-poverty support. That includes things like TANF, Foster Care & Adoption, and Energy & Utility Aid, and other related programs. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Earned Income Tax Credit
categories.desc: Refundable tax credit that boosts earnings for lower-income workers

categories.title: Refundable EITC
categories.desc: Refundable earned income credit payments
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on refundable earned income credit payments. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Administration & Compliance
categories.desc: IRS administration and compliance associated with the EITC
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Earned Income Tax Credit behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. The amount here depends more on workload, staffing, technology, and oversight needs than on direct payments to the public.

This part of the budget covers refundable tax credit that boosts earnings for lower-income workers. That includes things like Refundable EITC and Administration & Compliance. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

categories.title: Housing Assistance
categories.desc: Federal rental assistance and housing support for low-income households
categories.details: This part of the budget helps lower-income households afford rent and stay housed. It includes vouchers, project-based subsidies, public housing, and other housing support. The total here depends mostly on how many households get help, local housing costs, and the rules of each program.

categories.title: Housing Choice Vouchers
